title: fstats_copy computes px = width * height without overflow guard, then indexes into pixels up to px*4 bytes
description: The exported WASM function `fstats_copy(pixels, width, height)` computes `px = width * height` using plain Rust `usize` multiplication (line 2488). On wasm32 `usize` is 32 bits; a caller passing width=65536 and height=65536 overflows to px=0, making the loop a no-op. More dangerously, values like width=1<<16, height=1<<15 give px=0x8000_0000 which as `px*4` wraps to 0 — but the loop bounds come from `px` so the actual read comes from `pixels.len()` guard inside `fs_core_scalar`. The real risk is that `fs_core_scalar` indexes `d[i]` through `d[i+3]` for `i` up to `(px-1)*4`; if `pixels.len() < px*4` due to the overflow, Rust's slice indexing will panic (not UB), but the width/height values are fully JS-controlled with no validation. Compare with `frame_stats` (line 2819) which uses `width.saturating_mul(height)`. This inconsistency means `fstats_copy` does not have the same overflow protection.

category: unsafe memory access / potential out-of-bounds read
title: fs_core_simd uses unsafe v128_load without alignment or bounds comment; relies on caller contract that px*4 <= d.len()
description: Both `fs_core_simd` (line 2522) and `fs_core_simd_exact` (line 2639) use `unsafe { v128_load(d.as_ptr().add(i) as *const v128) }` where `i = c * 16`. The load reads 16 bytes; the loop runs for `chunks = px / 4` iterations. If any caller passes a slice shorter than `px * 4` bytes the load reads past the end of the slice — undefined behavior in Rust unsafe. In practice the two callers are `fstats_scalar` / `fstats_simd` (which use the resident buffer filled to `w*h*4`) and the production `frame_stats` (which guards with `limit = pixels.len().min(expected)` and takes the truncated path when `limit != expected`). However there is no in-function assert; the safety relies purely on call-site discipline. A future caller or refactor that passes a shorter-than-expected slice would silently cause UB.

title: PerceptualComparer::new computes n = width * height without overflow guard before allocating n*4 bytes
description: The WASM-exported `PerceptualComparer::new(ref_rgba, width, height)` computes `let n = width * height` (line 2265) then allocates `vec![0u8; n * 4]` for the scratch buffer. On wasm32, both multiplications are unchecked 32-bit operations. A caller passing width=0x8000_0000 and height=2 would compute n=0 (overflow) and allocate an empty scratch buffer. Subsequent `all_at(len)` calls with a non-zero len would then slice an empty buffer, panicking. More critically, `input_ptr(len)` would resize to `len` so the scratch grows correctly — but the initial allocation and the inner `PerceptualCore::new(ref_rgba, width, height, ...)` may also perform unchecked arithmetic on the attacker-controlled dimensions. The ref_rgba length is not cross-checked against width*height before constructing PerceptualCore.

category: JSON injection via unescaped string fields
title: rows_to_json interpolates file name and decode_strategy strings into JSON without escaping quotes or control characters
description: The manual JSON serializer in `rows_to_json` interpolates `row.file` (line 206) and `row.decode_strategy` (line 222) directly into JSON string literals using `format!("..."{}")`. The only sanitization is replacing backslash with forward-slash in `row.file`. A filename containing a double-quote character — which is legal on Linux/macOS filesystems — would produce malformed JSON (e.g. `{"file": "foo"bar.orf", ...}`). Since the output file (`benchmark/results_native.json`) is consumed by downstream tools and potentially checked into source control, a crafted filename could corrupt the JSON or inject extra fields. This is a local developer tool so exploitability is limited, but the pattern is still incorrect.

category: unsafe pointer arithmetic — missing bounds proof
title: v_pass_tiled uses raw pointer arithmetic with a SAFETY comment that understates the actual precondition
description: The `// SAFETY: tile, x0, yi validated by caller contract + clamp; bench synthetic data.` comment (line 124) is misleading: the clamp only bounds `yi` to `[0, h-1]`; it does not prevent `row + x0 * 3 + tile * 3` from exceeding `src.len()`. If `w` and `h` are constructed so that `x1 = (x0 + TILE).min(w)` still causes `(yi * w + x1 - 1) * 3 + 2 >= src.len()`, the pointer reads past the allocation. In practice the benchmark always creates `src` of exactly `w * h * 3` elements and this is a developer-only binary with hardcoded synthetic data, so real exploitability is nil. The finding is that the `unsafe` block lacks a formal invariant proof and could silently become UB if caller-provided `w`, `h`, or kernel size change.

title: WASM public API functions accept floating-point look parameters with no range validation before passing to the pipeline
description: The exported functions `process_orf`, `process_dng`, `process_cr2`, and `apply_look` accept floating-point parameters (exposure_ev, contrast, highlights, shadows, etc.) and `output_flags` (u32 bitmask) from JavaScript with no range validation. While `wb_r`/`wb_b` are clamped to `min(8.0)` and checked for finite/positive values, the other parameters (e.g. `exposure_ev`, `saturation`, `clarity`) are passed directly to `apply_look_params` without bounds checking. An adversarial caller could pass `f32::INFINITY`, `f32::NAN`, or extreme values that propagate through tone math and produce garbled but not-panicking output. Adding a validation layer at the WASM boundary (clamp or reject out-of-range look params) would harden against malformed callers and make the API contract explicit.

title: No evidence of cargo audit or dependency vulnerability scanning in the build pipeline
description: The codebase depends on wasm-bindgen, js-sys, web-sys, and (conditionally) libjxl via the jxl-ffi crate. There is no cargo-audit step visible in the repo build scripts. Adding `cargo audit` (or `cargo deny`) to CI would catch known CVEs in transitive dependencies before they reach production WASM artifacts served to browsers. This is a standard supply-chain hygiene measure for any WASM API that processes untrusted binary blobs.
code_excerpt: // No audit step found in build-parallel-wasm.ps1 or build-msvc.ps1
